title: The pre-sleep bedtime activity
subtitle: A fifteen-minute consistent wind-down routine for signalling sleep to the nervous system.
excerpt: A fifteen-minute pre-sleep wind-down routine for nights when sleep feels far away or elusive. The same five actions, in the same order, each night for one week. The consistency is the practice, not the actions themselves, but the body learning that this sequence means sleep is coming.
sourceNotes: A consistent pre-sleep routine as a sleep-hygiene practice is documented across sleep medicine research (CBT-I, sleep hygiene guidelines from the British Sleep Society). The specific five-action form used here is adapted from Day 1 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

content.text: A fifteen-minute pre-sleep wind-down routine for one week. The same five actions, in the same order, at the same time each night. The consistency is the practice, not the actions themselves, but the nervous system learning that this sequence means sleep is coming. The body stops guessing when sleep will arrive if the same signals arrive every night.
Choose a target bedtime. Start the routine fifteen minutes before it. Run these five actions in order, every night:

Phone face-down or in another room. Laptop closed. Television off. This is the hardest step for most people. Don't negotiate. Screens off, fifteen minutes before sleep. This is the signal the routine starts.
Open the window briefly if the room is warm. Make the bed if it isn't made. Put out tomorrow's clothes if that's useful. The room is ready to receive you.
Three minutes of gentle movement: slow neck rolls, shoulder drops, a forward fold if you can. Not exercise, just enough movement for the body to know the day is finished and the rest is beginning.

In a notebook by the bed: write one line about the day. Not a review, not a plan, not an emotion, one factual line. "Had a long meeting, walked at lunch, called Mum." The day is noted. The notebook can hold it through the night.
Lie down. Lights off. The routine is complete. Let the visualise-the-sleep-space practice carry you from here if needed.

Run the same five steps in the same order every night for seven days, including weekends. The body learns the signal pattern fastest when the pattern is consistent. Nights when you skip a step or do the steps out of order restart the learning. Seven consecutive nights before reviewing whether the routine is working.

A steady pre-sleep routine is a standard sleep-hygiene practice. It shows up in sleep medicine research, including CBT for Insomnia (CBT-I) protocols and the British Sleep Society's guidelines. The five-action form used here is adapted from Day 1 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
